titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Mount Ziyang

div.head: 嵫陽山

figure.head: Mount Ziyang to the west of the county seat, published in 13b, 14a
嵫陽山位置示意圖；刊於清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》葉13b和14a

div.p: 嵫陽山石刻同尖山和陽山石刻一樣，皆毀於採石。故而被遺忘殆盡，近年只有張總提及。見下文“討論”部分。
嵫陽山“大空王佛”刻銘拓本僅存臺北中央研究院。此佛名在山東刻經山已知多達15處。此拓還有八個經主名。該拓本此前誤歸爲尖山刻銘，今已確認實屬於於嵫陽山。

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 由於開採石灰岩，嵫陽山今已幾乎夷爲平地。據當地人口述，開採岩石發生在20世紀50年代。衛星照片顯示，今嵫陽山僅有寬500米左右的馬蹄形山石。其地理位置是東經116.660590度，北緯35.546244度，相當於UTM坐標系統第50S帶，東行469235，北行3933675。該址位於兗州以西15公里，鄒城西北32公里，以及濟寧市東北偏北16公里。
刻銘的確切位置已無從得知。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》詳細記載了嵫陽山舊貌：嵫山，在城西南三十里，高一里，廣三里。縣得名以此山。自東北起一峰，迤邐而西，斷而復續。至西又起一峰，形若覆盂，迴環顧盻，殊有逸致。清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第27a頁。

金石文獻記録嵫陽山1.2號和1.3號刻銘的地理位置有兩説。馮雲鵬（1765－1839）和馮雲鵷（1779－1857）兄弟編纂《金石索》（1821－1823）曰：“横列嵫陽山南嶺之頂面。”《金石索》第4册：第787頁。 《滋陽縣誌》（1888）則載録：“在嵫山中峰石上。”清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第99a頁。 這兩説實則未必抵觸，可能是從不同角度觀看所致。如果互相補充，嵫陽山刻銘很可能位於南面山麓中央的頂峰。
上述兩種金石文獻皆著録嵫陽山2號刻銘位於東面頂，即鳳凰嶺。《金石索》第4册：第788頁。清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第99a頁。

div.head: 地名
div.p: 清康熙十一年（1672）刻本《嵫陽縣誌》稱其爲“磁山”，見清康熙十一年（1672）刻本《嵫陽縣誌》卷四下，第91a頁，張元冶（17世紀中葉）作《嵫陽縣誌跋後》。 或許因其可能有磁鐵礦石；又稱作“嵫山”。《金石索》寫作“嵫陽山”。《金石索》第4册：第787頁。上引清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》則稱爲“嵫山”。縣治在此山以南，因此得名。清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第27a頁。至晚在清代末期，該山以縣城得名。1962年，嵫陽改稱兗州。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 嵫陽山的寺院原址今已不得而知，據《兗州府誌》（隆慶六年［1572］和萬曆元年［1573］序）記載，元代（1271－1368）至正年間（1341－1367）建有洪福寺。 明萬曆刻本《兗州府誌》卷四，第709頁。距嵫陽山最近的大型寺院爲兗州隆興寺，原名普樂寺。明萬曆刻本《兗州府誌》 卷四，第706頁。該寺是當地最重要的寺院，是隋文帝（581－604年在位）供奉舍利的113座寺院之一（其中有15座位於山東）。負責護送舍利到此的僧人法性是兗州人。道宣（596－667）編纂於唐貞觀十九年（645）的《續高僧傳》載：釋法性，兗州人。……仁壽之年，勅召送舍利於本州普樂寺。《續高僧傳》T#2060，50：675a10－21。 此事發生在隋仁壽二年（602），據説爲建寺之年。明萬曆刻本《兗州府誌》卷四，第706頁。游自勇第28頁。該寺址很有可能在此之前已是宗教中心。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 拓本“大空王佛”佛名之旁有八個供養人名。這些人名根據宗教身份分爲兩組，僧人之名（嵫陽山1.2號）刻於佛名之下，居士之名（嵫陽山1.3號）則於佛名右側。
四位僧人的名字有僧鳳、僧萇和道懷等三個清晰可辨。僧鳳（559－650）之名見於《續高僧傳》：以般若爲心田，以涅槃爲意得。講《法華經》百有餘遍。《續高僧傳》T#2060，50：527a3－4。 僧鳳長居於京師長安，但似有可能早年活動於山東。
居士耿紹宗（生卒不詳）及其妻高氏和兒子子遠之名刻於佛名右側最顯著的位置，這或許因他出資最豐厚。耿氏及其子的名字未見於任何文獻。其側還刻有三個字的佛主名，但已無法釋讀。
另外，位於東峰頂嵫陽山2號刻銘的“天水山人”，也無從查考。

div.head: 斷代
div.p: 嵫陽山刻銘無確鑿紀年。根據書法風格，可能刻於6世紀下半葉。佛名的筆法可對照書院東山 《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第335－338頁。和雲翠山 《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷 第369－372頁。所刻相同佛名。 賴非認爲，嵫陽山刻銘爲僧安道壹早年之作。 2016年9月10日於北京房山會議上的個人交流。

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 有三位清代金石學家著録記載了嵫陽山刻銘，僅涉及嵫陽山1.2號、1.3號及2號，未提及重要的“大空王佛”佛名（嵫陽山1.1號）。最早的著録見於馮雲鵬和馮雲鵷的《金石索》。此書是馮雲鵷任嵫陽知縣時編纂的，刊刻於清道光元年至三年（1821－1823）。當時，馮氏意識到嵫陽山刻銘的重要，下令嚴禁開山採石。清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第115a頁。馮氏兄弟明確記載了刻銘的位置，並將可辨識的字摹刻刷印，收入書中。但是，他們改變了刻銘的佈局，因此無法認出刻字之間原本的位置關係。他們提供刻字的尺寸，並提到綫刻蓮花環繞刻銘。通過與鄒城崗山及尖山刻銘相較，他們將嵫陽山刻銘的年代定爲同屬北朝。《金石索》第4册：第787－788頁。
馮雲鵬還爲刻經址作詩，其中一首一則收録在他的《掃紅亭吟稿》，描述尋訪刻經址之不易：

quote.p: 滑石難登顧步遲，何處騎驢空引領。
剔蘚驚看古隸横，佛主僧萇僧鳳等。《掃紅亭吟稿》第412a頁。另一首略述該址的詩作可見《金石索》第4册：第787頁。

div.p: 除了詩作，馮氏還在題註提到：“惜半壁已被人鑿去，不知年月。”有關馮雲鵬對此所作之完整記述，見下文“討論”部分，。這或許是他未見佛名的原因。
第二則著録見於吴式芬（1796－1856）撰寫的《金石彙目分編》。吴氏踏訪此地，並爲嵫陽山1.2號及嵫陽山2號題記撰寫了條目，内容簡略，所述與《金石索》一致。
第三則著録見於清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》，除刻銘位置所記略異，其餘都接近《金石索》。另外，它指出周圍的圖案，除了蓮花之外還有鹿，並粗略推斷其爲“唐以前書”。清光緒十四年（1888）刻本《滋陽縣誌》第99a頁。

三則著録均未提及佛名，儘管其撰寫人都有可能親自造訪過嵫陽山。令人訝異的是，在19與20世紀之交，傑出的金石學家陸增祥（1833－1889）之子陸繼煇（1839－1905）纂《八瓊室金石補正續編》，嵫陽山1.2號和1.3號題記忽然與佛名（嵫陽山1.1號）一起出現。此書出版於陸繼煇去世後的民國十四年（1925）。但陸繼煇將此刻銘歸屬鄒城尖山。《八瓊室金石補正續編》 第899册：第388a頁。顯然他本人未曾到過尖山，也未造訪嵫陽山。他根據所見拓本，提供録文、刻字確切的尺寸，以及嵫陽山1.2號和1.3號刻銘的位置關係。不同於馮氏兄弟與吴式芬，他稱此刻銘爲正書，而非隸書。
金山學家劉聲木（1876－1959）也提到佛名並同樣誤記嵫陽山1.1號至1.3號刻銘位於尖山，他顯然也未曾親臨其地。他在其《續補寰宇訪碑録》（1929），認爲佛名看似破壞了僧名的刻字，應爲後來加刻（嵫陽山1.2號）。《續補寰宇訪碑録》 第20345頁。
劉聲木的判斷似乎基於現存臺北中央研究院一套分作三段的拓片（）。這是嵫陽山刻銘目前所知僅存拓本。第二段拓本（12493－3）的背面有一條題籖，云：尖山佛主僧鳳□等殘題字。正書，摩崖，無年月。碑文爲加刻“大空王佛” 四字所掩，三録未載。
“三録”，即劉聲木自序所列繆荃孫（1844－1919）《寰宇訪碑録》、趙之謙（1829－1884）《補寰宇訪碑録》及羅振玉（1866－1940）《再續寰宇訪碑録》。此段内容除了最後一句劉氏記爲“繆氏失載”外，與劉聲木所記幾乎一致。參見下文嵫陽山1.2號“討論”部分。因此，這條題籖無法判定是編目人員根據劉氏所録而作，還是劉氏收藏這幅拓本時題寫的。
張總着意《金石索》的著録，傳録了該書摹刻的佛主名並指出該處曾刻有佛號。張總 第7－8頁和第34頁。張總第101和112頁，註14。

div.head: 總目
div.p: 今知嵫陽山有兩處石刻。其一，佛名“大空王佛”（嵫陽山1.1號）及其周圍的兩則題記（嵫陽山1.2號和1.3號）。其二，一條書家題款及僅知的四個互不相聯的刻字（嵫陽山2號）。

div.head: 嵫陽山1.1號
div.p: 發現嵫陽山佛名刻銘之後，已知山東目前所存“大空王佛”增至15例。列表詳見。討論見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第60－63頁以及本卷。此佛名使嵫陽山成爲山東最引人注目的刻經址之一。

劉聲木認爲佛名覆部分佛主姓名之上，但如果仔細觀察臺北中央研究院僅存的拓本，此無法成立。 展示佛名的整體情况，此圖乃由三段拓本的照片拼合而成。劉聲木或許因此認爲“佛”字最後一竪劃延伸到拓本最下面的部分，並因此覆蓋了第一行僧人的名字。其實不然，拓本細部（）顯示“佛”字最後一竪筆劃並未延伸到下面的刻字，下方痕迹實因岩石表面風化且拓本重裱時拉扯變形。因此，佛名其實並未覆蓋佛主名字。這表明佛名並非19世紀添加，而是與佛主名字一同刊刻。

div.head: 嵫陽山1.2號和嵫陽山1.3號
div.p: 嵫陽山1.2號刻銘包括供養佛名的僧人佛主名，其他資料信息則無從查考。嵫陽山1.3號刻銘的居士供養人名同樣也没有更具體的資料。

div.head: 嵫陽山2號
div.p: 根據 《金石索》的著録，嵫陽山2號刻銘個别的刻字包括“幽山”“頂”及“鉅”，這些字似乎出自描繪山景的文字。其作者兼書者署名“天水山人”，顯示其愛好山水之心。

div.head: 刊刻技術
div.p: 嵫陽山刻銘今已盡毀，其刊刻技法無從查考。但是根據嵫陽山1.1號刻銘拓本“空”及“王”字受損的輪廓來看，隱約可見題刻風化嚴重。這或許是馮氏兄弟注意到了保存較好的小字題記，却忽略大字佛名的原因。

div.head: Mount Ziyang
div.p: The engravings on Mount Ziyang 嵫陽山, like those on Mount Jian 尖山 and Mount Yang 陽山, have been destroyed by quarrying. Consequently they were forgotten. In recent times, Zhang Zong 張總 alone has noted them. See Discussion below.

A single rubbing has been located in the Academia Sinica in Taipei, with the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛), which occurs as many as fifteen times on the Shandong mountains. The rubbing also includes the names of eight donors. In the past, this rubbing was identified as of an inscription at Mount Jian. It has now been established that the engraving was located on Mount Ziyang.

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Ziyang is a limestone outcrop, whose crest has been almost completely removed by quarrying. According to locals, this assault took place in the 1950s. Seen from above, only rocky spars in the shape of a horseshoe, about 500 meters wide, remain. Geographically it is situated 116.660590° east and 35.546244° north, WGS84. This equals 469235 E (easting) and 3933675 N (northing), UTM zone 50S. The site is located 15 km west of the city of Yanzhou 兗州, 32 km northwest of the city of Zoucheng 鄒城, and 16 km north-northeast of Ji­ning 濟寧.
The original positions of the inscriptions are unknown.

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p:
The Gazetteer of Ziyang County (Ziyang Xianzhi 滋陽縣誌) of 1888 gives a detailed description of the erstwhile topography: Mount Zi[yang] is located 30 miles southwest of the city. It is one mile high, and three miles wide. The mountain gave its name to the county seat. From the northeast rises a peak which stretches and winds westwards. It halts and then continues again. To the west, another peak rises. Like a basin upside down, it turns back on itself, extraordinary indeed. 嵫山，在城西南三十里，高一里，廣三里。縣得名以此山。自東北起一峰，迤邐而西，斷而復續。至西又起一峰，形若覆盂，迴環顧盻，殊有逸致。
27a.

The epigraphic records give two different locations for the inscriptions, ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 1.3. The Examination of Epigraphica (Jinshi suo 金石索) of 1821–1823 by the two brothers, Feng Yunpeng 馮雲鵬 (1765–1839) and Feng Yunyuan 馮雲鵷 (1779–1857) described them as “being placed horizontally on top of the southern ridge of Mount Ziyang”. “横列嵫陽山南嶺之頂面［……］。” 4: 787. The Gazetteer of Ziyang County of 1888 locates them “on the rock of the middle peak”. “在嵫山中峰石上［……］。” 99a. These two descriptions do not necessarily contradict one another, but may result from looking at the mountain from different viewpoints. If, indeed, they complement each other, it is possible that the inscriptions were located on the central peak on the southern ridge.
Both epigraphic records agree that another inscription, ZYS 2, was at the summit of the eastern peak, the Phoenix Ridge (Fenghuangling 鳳凰嶺). 4: 788. 99a.

div.head: Place Name
div.p:
The Gazetteer of Ziyang County of 1672 names the mountain Mount Magnet (Ci Shan 磁山),See the postscript by Zhang Yuanye 張元冶 (mid-17th century) in scroll 4, second part: 91a.
perhaps on account of a possible magnetite deposit beneath it. The Gazetteer also gives an alternative name, Mount Zi (Zi Shan 嵫山). The Examination of Epigraphica of 1821–1823 uses the name Mount Ziyang 嵫陽山. 4: 787. The Gazetteer of Ziyang County of 1888 simply refers to Mount Zi (Zi Shan 嵫山), as cited above, and says that the county seat, Ziyang 嵫陽, is located on the southern side of Mount Zi. 27a. Hence its name. Today, the usual name is Mount Ziyang 嵫陽山. At least by the 19th century, the name of the city was given to the mountain. In 1962, the city of Ziyang 嵫陽, was renamed Yan­zhou 兗州.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: The site of the original monastery at Mount Ziyang can no longer be identified. The Gazetteer of Yan­zhou Prefecture (Yanzhou Fuzhi 兗州府誌), with prefaces of 1572 and 1573, records that a Hongfu Monastery 洪福寺 was at Mount Ziyang, founded in the zhizheng 至正 era (1341–1367) of the Yuan dynasty (1271–1368). 4: 709. The nearest large monastery was Long­xing Monastery 隆興寺 in Yanzhou, originally named Pule Monastery 普樂寺. 4: 706. It was the most important monastery in the prefecture. It was chosen as one of the approximately 113 monasteries across the empire (15 of them in Shandong) to which Emperor Wen of Sui 隋文帝 (r. 581–604) distributed the Buddha’s relics. The monk Faxing 法性, who brought the relics to the Monastery, was a native of Yanzhou. The Sequel to the Biographies of Eminent Monks (Xu Gaoseng zhuan 續高僧傳) by Daoxuan 道宣 (596–667), completed in 645, records: “Monk Faxing came from Yanzhou. … During the years of the renshou era [601–604], he received the order to take the Buddha’s relics to Pule Monastery in his native prefecture.” “釋法性，兗州人。［……］仁壽之年，勅召送舍利於本州普樂寺［……］。” T#2060, 50: 675a10–21. This took place in 602, the year in which the Monastery is said to have been founded. 4: 706. See also 28. It is very likely that there was an earlier religious center at the site.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: Around the Buddha name on the rubbing are names of eight donors. They are spatially divided into two groups, according to their religious status. The names of the clerics, ZYS 1.2, are placed beneath the Buddha name, while those of the lay persons, ZYS 1.3, are to the left.

Among the names of the four clerics, three are legible: Sengfeng 僧鳳, Sengchang 僧萇, and Daohuai 道懷. A monk named Sengfeng appears in the Sequel to the Biographies of Eminent Monks: The Perfection of Wisdom literature was his frame of mind, from the Nirvana Sutra he took his intentions, and the Lotus Sutra he preached more than a 100 times. 以般若爲心田，以涅槃爲意得。講《法華經》百有餘遍。 T#2060, 50: 527a3–4. This Monk Sengfeng 僧鳳 (559–650) was based at the capital Chang’an, 長安, yet it is an intriguing possibility that, in his early years, he was active in Shandong.

As the name of the lay donor, Geng Shaozong 耿紹宗 (dates unknown), is placed, together with those of his wife Gao 高 and his son Ziyuan 子遠, in the most prominent position immediately to the left of the Buddha name, it is likely that he provided most of the funds. Neither father nor son can be identified in historical records. Next to these names was another donor name in three characters, but it cannot now be read.
The Mountain Man of the Heavenly Water (Tianshui shanren 天水山人) in the inscription, ZYS 2, on the eastern peak, cannot be identified, either.

div.head: Dating
div.p:
No evidence for an exact dating of the engravings survives. According to the calligraphic style, they probably were carved in the second half of the sixth century. The brush work of the Buddha name is comparable with that of the same name carved at East Mount Shuyuan 書院東山
335–338. and Mount Yuncui 雲翠山. 369–372. Informally, Lai Fei has suggested that the engraving at Ziyang was an early work by Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562–579).Personal communication on September 10, 2016, in Fangshan near Beijing.

div.head: Research History
div.p: Three nineteenth century epigraphers documented the engravings at Mount Ziyang, but they only recorded the colophons ZYS 1.2, ZYS 1.3 and ZYS 2. None of them mentioned the main inscription, the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛; ZYS 1.1). The earliest record is in the Examination of Epigraphica of 1821–1823 by Feng Yunpeng and Feng Yunyuan. When editing their book, Feng Yunyuan was the County Magistrate (Zhixian 知縣) of Ziyang County. Having observed the severe destruction on the mountain, and being aware of the importance of the inscriptions, he issued a decree prohibiting further quarrying. 115a. In their book, the brothers specified the location of the inscriptions and reproduced the legible characters in woodblock print as if it were a rubbing. However, they made changes in the layout of the inscriptions, whose original spatial relations are thus not recognizable. The authors further gave measurements of the characters and mentioned drawings of lotus ornaments that surrounded them. By comparing the calligraphy with that of the inscriptions at Mounts Gang 崗山 and Jian 尖山 in Zoucheng, they dated the engravings at Mount Ziyang to the same period. 4: 787–788.
Feng Yunpeng also wrote poems on the site. One, in his Drafts of Chants at Sweeping Red [Leaves] Pavilion (Saohongting yin gao 掃紅亭吟稿) evokes the challenging access to the inscriptions:

quote.p: …On the face of the cliff are finely carved lotus stalks.
The slippery rocks are difficult to climb; slowly, as the steps slip backward. Which way goes my donkey, drawn and led in vain?
As I scraped away the moss, I was taken aback to see ancient clerical script sprawled out there: "Donors of the Buddha [name] Sengchang, Sengfeng, and others.”

p.foreign: 滑石難登顧步遲，何處騎驢空引領。
剔蘚驚看古隸横，佛主僧萇僧鳳等。
p.note: 412a. Another poem with less information is found in the Examination of Epigraphica. 4: 787.

div.p: In addition to these verses, Feng comments in a note on the inscription itself, stating that “regrettably, half of the rock wall has been cut away. No date is known 惜半壁已被人鑿去，不知年月。” For Feng Yunpeng’s entire footnote, see Discussion, below, p. . This could be the reason why he did not see a Buddha name there.

The second epigraphic record is found in the Categorized Edition of an Assembled Catalog of Epigraphica (Jinshi huimu fenbian 金石彙目分編) by Wu Shifen 吴式芬 (1796–1856). After visiting the site, he wrote entries on the colophons, ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 2. His concise information is consistent with that in the Examination of Epigraphica.

The third record is in the Gazetteer of Ziyang County of 1888. The documentation is similar to that in the Examination of Epigraphica, but the Gazetteer has somewhat different information about the location; it says that the ornament includes not only lotuses, but also deer, and gives the dating simply as “before the Tang dynasty.” 99a.

None of these records mentions a Buddha name, although all three authors probably visited the mountain. Surprisingly, around the turn of the century, the colophons, ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 1.3, suddenly appeared together with the Buddha name, ZYS 1.1, in the Sequel to the Supplementary Corrigenda to the Epigraphica of Eight Gems Studio (Baqiong shi jinshi buzheng xubian 八瓊室金石補正續編) by Lu Jihui 陸繼煇 (1839–1905), the son of the eminent epigrapher Lu Zengxiang 陸增祥 (1833–1889). This work was published posthumously in 1925. Lu Jihui mistakenly assigned the inscriptions to Mount Jian in Zoucheng. 899: 388a. Clearly, he visited neither Mount Jian, nor Mount Ziyang. Basing himself on rubbings, he gave transcriptions, exact measurements of the characters, and the relative positions of ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 1.3. Unlike the Feng brothers and Wu Shifen, who describes the calligraphy as clerical script (lishu 隸書), Lu Jihui calls it standard script (zhengshu 正書).

Liu Shengmu 劉聲木 (1876–1959), who did not personally know the site either, was the second epigrapher to mention the Buddha name and to assign the inscriptions ZYS 1.1–1.3 to Mount Jian. In his Sequel to the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Xubu Huanyu fangbei lu 續補寰宇訪碑録) of 1929, he suggested that the Buddha name was added later, as it had damaged part of the names of the clerical donors (ZYS 1.2). 20345.

It is likely that Liu Shengmu based his information on the set of three rubbings that is now preserved in the Academia Sinica in Taibei (fig. ). Indeed, no other rubbings of the inscriptions at Mount Ziyang are known. On the back of the second rubbing (12493-3) is a handwritten note: Fragment of an inscription of the donor of the Buddha [name], Sengfeng [...] and others on Mount Jian. Polished cliff inscription in standard script; undated. The stele [engraved] text has been covered by the added inscription of the four characters “Buddha King of Great Emptiness”. The three epigraphica did not record it. 尖山佛主僧鳳□等殘題字。正書，摩崖，無年月。碑文爲加刻“大空王佛”四字所掩，三録未載。

“The three epigraphica” which Liu Shengmu lists in his preface are the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Huanyu fangbei lu 寰宇訪碑録) by Miao Quansun 繆荃孫 (1844–1919), the Supplements to the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Bu Huanyu fangbei lu 補寰宇訪碑録) by Zhao Zhiqian 趙之謙 (1829–1884), and More Addenda to the Record of Enquiries into Stelae in the Whole World (Zai xu Huang­yu fangbei lu 再續寰宇訪碑録) by Luo Zhenyu 羅振玉 (1866–1940). The above note is almost identical with Liu Shengmu’s record, except for the last sentence, which reads: “Miao [Quansun] failed to record it 繆氏失載。” See also Discussion below of ZYS 1.2. Thus it was either written by a bibliographer basing himself on Liu Shengmu’s record, or Liu Shengmu once owned the rubbing and added the note himself.According to the information given by the librarian at the Academia Sinica, the question cannot be solved.

Zhang Zong noted the record in the Examination of Epigraphica, transcribing the donors’ names according to it; he also established that one or more Buddha names had been carved as well. 7–8 and 34. 101 and 112, note 14.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: Two inscriptions at Mount Ziyang are known. One is the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” (ZYS 1.1) with two colophons (ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 1.3). The other is a signature of a calligrapher and four disparate characters (ZYS 2).

div.head: ZYS 1.1
div.p: With the identification of the inscriptions recorded by the epigraphers as being at Mount Ziyang, the numbers of the name “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” in Shandong is presently thought to be fifteen.For a list see the present volume, . For a discussion see 82–87, and p. in the present volume. This name now draws Mount Ziyang into the most conspicuous group of sites in Shandong.

Liu Shengmu’s statement that the characters of the Buddha name covered over parts of the donor’s names does not stand up to scrutiny of the only extant rubbings preserved in Academia Sinica in Taibei. A comprehensive overview of the Buddha name in which the photographs of the three rubbings have been stitched into one image is shown in figure . Liu Shengmu may have had the impression that the last vertical stroke of the character fo 佛 extended to the lowest part of the rubbing, and thus covered the first column of the monks’ names. In fact, details (fig. ) reveal that the last stroke of the character fo 佛 was not extended. The impression of traces beneath the stroke may have resulted from the weathered surface of the rock, and from the possibility that the rubbings were deformed when they were remounted. Thus, no stroke of the Buddha name covered the colophons. This evidence of the rubbings makes it clear that the Buddha name was not added in the nineteenth century, but was carved together with the donors’ names.

div.head: ZYS 1.2 and ZYS 1.3
div.p: The inscription ZYS 1.2 contains the names of the clerical donors for the Buddha name. No further information can be found. The same applies to the lay donors in inscription ZYS 1.3.

div.head: ZYS 2
div.p:
The disparate characters of ZYS 2, as recorded in the Examination of Epigraphica, include “tranquil mountain” (you shan 幽山), “peak” (ding 頂) and “hard iron” (ju
鉅). They seem to be part of a description of the landscape, in which the author-calligrapher, the Mountain Man of Heavenly Water, expresses his attachment to mountains.

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: As the inscriptions at Mount Ziyang have been destroyed, the carving techniques cannot be examined. But the frayed contours of the characters kong 空 and wang 王 in ZYS 1.1 in the rubbings suggest that the inscription was heavily weathered and only faintly visible. This may explain why these huge characters were overlooked by Feng Yunpeng and Feng Yunyuan, whose eyes were drawn to the better preserved smaller characters of the colophons.
